elements.sent-2411-001: Modern recommendation systems often create information cocoons, limiting users' exposure to diverse content.
elements.sent-2411-002: To enhance user experience, a crucial challenge is developing systems that can balance content exploration and exploitation, allowing users to adjust their recommendation preferences.
elements.sent-2411-003: Intuitively, this balance can be achieved through a tree-structured representation, where depth search facilitates exploitation and breadth search enables exploration.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, current works face two challenges to achieve this target: (1) Euclidean methods fail to fully capture hierarchical structures and lack flexibility in balancing exploration-exploitation, while (2) hyperbolic approaches, despite better hierarchical modeling, suffer from insufficient semantic alignment due to their reliance on Euclidean text encoders.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address these challenges, we propose \model, a hyperbolic representation learning framework that jointly aligns user-item collaborative information with textual descriptions in hyperbolic space.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our framework introduces two key technique novelty: (1) a hierarchical-aware graph-llm alignment mechanism that enables better hierarchical representation, and (2) a hyperbolic hierarchical tree structure that facilitates user-adjustable exploration-exploitation trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-007: Extensive experiments demonstrate that \model consistently outperforms both Euclidean and hyperbolic baselines, achieving up to 5.49\% improvement in \acc metrics and 11.39\% increase in diversity metrics.
elements.sent-2411-008: As online platforms grow exponentially, users are overwhelmed by an influx of information, resulting in information cocoons that restrict their exposure to diverse and novel content~\cite{kunaver2017diversity, castells2021novelty .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Traditional recommendation approaches have primarily relied on collaborative filtering and interaction data~\cite{he2020lightgcn, koren2009matrix , where users with similar historical preferences are expected to share future interests~\cite{chen2021neural .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, these methods face two critical challenges in improving user experience.
elements.sent-2411-011: First, they often fail to capture the rich semantic relationships between items and evolving user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, a jazz enthusiast might wish to explore rock music, yet current models struggle to understand these semantic connections and adapt to such evolving preferences (as illustrated in Figure 1).
elements.sent-2411-013: Second, existing approaches inadequately capture the underlying hierarchical structure inherent in user-item networks~\cite{zheng2017hierarchical, unger2020hierarchical , a critical factor for improving performance and personalization.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The emergence of large language models (LLMs) offers a solution to the limitations of semantic understanding.
elements.sent-2411-015: However, integrating LLM-derived semantic features with traditional collaborative signals remains challenging, especially when capturing the hierarchical structure of user preferences and item relationships.
elements.sent-2411-016: Hyperbolic space has emerged as a promising solution to these issues.
elements.sent-2411-017: It is a powerful tool for modeling tree-like structures and power-law distributions~\cite{krioukov2010hyperbolic,sun2021hgcf,yang2022hrcf,yang2022hicf , where its exponential volume growth naturally aligns with hierarchical, scale-free structures~\cite{ganea2018hyperbolic, chami2019hyperbolic, liu2019hyperbolic, zhang2021lorentzian, yang2023kappahgcn .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: In hyperbolic space, distances increase exponentially from the origin, allowing popular items or exploratory users to be positioned closer to the origin, while niche items or users with focused interests spread toward the boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-019: This spatial configuration enables hyperbolic graph neural network (GNN) models to perceive hierarchical relationships effectively, enhancing recommendation performance~\cite{sun2021hgcf, yang2022hicf, yang2022hrcf .}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, current hyperbolic recommendation systems still face two key limitations.
elements.sent-2411-021: First, hyperbolic approaches often lack proper semantic alignment due to their dependence on Euclidean text encoders, which struggle to integrate within hyperbolic space.
elements.sent-2411-022: This mismatch leaves existing hyperbolic models vulnerable to noise in collaborative information, as they fail to capture semantic insights effectively, impacting their robustness and accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-023: Second, hyperbolic recommender systems lack a systematic approach to balancing exploration (introducing new content) and exploitation (focusing on existing preferences)~\cite{wilson2021balancing .}
elements.sent-2411-024: This limitation hinders their adaptability to users' evolving interests and reduces the diversity and relevance of recommended items.. Proposed Methods.
elements.sent-2411-025: To address these challenges, we propose \model, a hyperbolic representation learning framework that facilitates exploration-exploitation through an intuitive tree-structured representation, where depth search enables exploitation and breadth search allows exploration.
elements.sent-2411-026: Our framework introduces two key components: (1) a hierarchical-aware graph-llm alignment mechanism that enhances semantic understanding and reduces noise sensitivity by jointly aligning textual descriptions with user-item collaborative information in hyperbolic space, and (2) a hyperbolic hierarchical tree structure that enables user-adjustable exploration-exploitation trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-027: This tree structure organizes preferences hierarchically, with higher layers representing broader interests, allowing systematic navigation between exploration and exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-028: To validate the effectiveness of \model, we conducted extensive experiments across multiple dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-029: Our model consistently outperformed baselines in both \acc and diversity, marking a significant achievement as it is the first model to excel in both aspects simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-030: To further highlight the benefits of our hyperbolic alignment framework, we compared \model\ with feature-empowered baselines, where it demonstrated superior performance.
elements.sent-2411-031: Additionally, we conducted detailed analysis on head and tail items, showing that \model effectively boosts recommendations for tail items, thus enhancing diversity.
elements.sent-2411-032: An ablation study, along with a decoder module analysis, confirms \model's capability to leverage both semantic meaning and collaborative information, providing deeper insights into the advantages of our approach.. Contributions.
elements.sent-2411-033: To summarize, our primary contribution are in three aspects.
elements.sent-2411-034: The first is the proposed \model, a hyperbolic representation learning framework that effectively balances exploration and exploitation by integrating semantic and collaborative information, supported by theoretical analysis demonstrating the advantages of this integration in hyperbolic space.
elements.sent-2411-035: In addition, we design a novel mechanism enabling personalized control over the exploration-exploitation trade-off through a hierarchical tree structure in hyperbolic space, allowing users to dynamically adjust their recommendation preferences.
elements.sent-2411-036: Moreover, through extensive experiments evaluating both accuracy and diversity metrics, we demonstrate that \model achieves state-of-the-art performance across both criteria - achieving up to 5.49\% improvement in \acc metrics and 11.39\% increase in diversity metrics, making it the first model to excel in both criteria simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this section, we review commonly used graph-based collaborative filtering approaches, large language models (LLMs) for recommendation, and hyperbolic representation learning methods.. Graph Collaborative Filtering.
elements.sent-2411-038: Graph Collaborative Filtering has emerged as a dominant approach in recommender systems, with graph neural networks (GNNs) playing a central role.
elements.sent-2411-039: Unlike traditional collaborative filtering techniques, such as matrix factorization~\cite{koren2009matrix , GNNs capitalize on the relational structure inherent in user-item interactions, enabling a more nuanced understanding of complex dependencies.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Prominent graph-based methods, such as NGCF~\cite{wang2019neural , SGL~\cite{wu2021self , and LightGCN~\cite{he2020lightgcn , have been widely adopted and have consistently achieved state-of-the-art performance, significantly boosting recommendation accuracy.}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Nevertheless, most of these models are constrained to Euclidean space, which may fall short in capturing the underlying power-law distribution commonly seen in user-item networks.
elements.sent-2411-043: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used in recommender systems due to their strong capability in understanding textual information.
elements.sent-2411-044: Some approaches leverage LLMs directly for various downstream recommendation tasks~\cite{bao2023tallrec, li2023text, lyu2023llm, zheng2024adapting, zhang2023collm , while others utilize LLMs as an auxiliary tool for collaborative filtering~\cite{ren2024representation, xi2024towards, wei2024llmrec .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: However, both approaches often require substantial inference time and are computationally inefficient.
elements.sent-2411-046: In our \model, we use language models solely to enhance data quality, avoiding the need for repeated inference during each recommendation by relying on hyperbolic embeddings for the final output.. Hyperbolic Representation Learning.
elements.sent-2411-047: To address the limitations of Euclidean space in modeling hierarchical structures, researchers have investigated the use of hyperbolic space~\cite{chami2019hyperbolic, liu2019hyperbolic, yang2022hrcf, zhang2021lorentzian , where distances grow exponentially as embeddings move away from the origin.}
elements.sent-2411-048: This property makes hyperbolic geometry particularly well-suited for modeling user-item interactions by more accurately capturing latent hierarchies and dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-049: Consequently, hyperbolic representation learning has gained considerable attention in the recommender systems domain~\cite{ying2018graph, vinh2020hyperml, sun2021hgcf, yang2022hicf , demonstrating improved performance over traditional Euclidean models.}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, none of these hyperbolic models fully leverage the rich semantic information embedded in language-based datasets.
elements.sent-2411-051: While some approaches incorporate knowledge graphs as supplementary information~\cite{tai2021knowledge, du2022hakg , these models primarily focus on refining hierarchical structures rather than achieving a deeper semantic understanding of each item or user.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Embeddings learned from the above graph collaborative filtering are by default in the Euclidean space, which is unable to capture tree-like structures.
elements.sent-2411-053: To address the problem, we introduce how to derive these embeddings in a hyperbolic space.. Riemannian Manifold.
elements.sent-2411-054: A Riemannian manifold $(\mathcal{M , g)$ is a smooth manifold that features an inner product $g_x : T_x\mathcal{M \times T_x\mathcal{M \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ at each point $x \in \mathcal{M}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: This inner product, known as the Riemannian metric, defines geometric characteristics of the space such as angles and curve lengths.
elements.sent-2411-056: The curvature of Riemannian manifolds results in different types of geometries: elliptic geometry with positive curvature, Euclidean geometry with zero curvature, and hyperbolic geometry with negative curvature.
elements.sent-2411-057: In this work, we focus on hyperbolic geometry, characterized by negative curvature.
elements.sent-2411-058: Hyperbolic space can be described by several equivalent models, each offering unique properties while being mathematically equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-059: The Lorentz model, also referred to as the hyperboloid model, is one of the commonly used representations of hyperbolic space.. Lorentz Model.
elements.sent-2411-060: An $n$-dimensional Lorentz manifold with negative curvature $-1/\kappa$ $(\kappa>0)$ is described as the Riemannian manifold $(\mathbb{H ^n_\kappa, g_\mathcal{L )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Here, $\mathbb{H ^n_\kappa = \{x \in \mathbb{R ^{n+1 : \langle x, x \rangle_\mathcal{L = -\kappa, x_0 > 0\}$, $g_\mathcal{L = \eta$ with $\eta = \mathbf{I _n$ except $\eta_{0,0 = -1$, and $\langle \cdot, \cdot \rangle_\mathcal{L}$ represents the Lorentzian inner product, which is defined as by \begin{equation}\langle x, y \rangle_\mathcal{L} := -x_0 y_0 + \sum_{i=1}^n x_i y_i.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-062: A tangent space is an $n$-dimensional vector space that approximates Euclidean space to hyperbolic space.
elements.sent-2411-063: Specifically, given a point $x \in \mathbb{H _\kappa^n$, there exists a tangent space $\mathcal{T _x\mathbb{H _\kappa^n$ that approximates $\mathbb{H _\kappa^n$: \begin{equation}\mathcal{T}_x\mathbb{H}_\kappa^n := \{v \in \mathbb{R}^{n+1} : \langle v, x \rangle_\mathcal{L} = 0\}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: The tangent space is defined to be orthogonal to $x$ with respect to the Lorentzian inner product, therefore making the approximation accurate at the reference point.
elements.sent-2411-065: We also need to define transitions between $\mathbb{H _\kappa^n$ and $\mathcal{T _x\mathbb{H _\kappa^n$, which give rise to the exponential and logarithmic maps.}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: For $x \in \mathbb{H _\kappa^n$ and $v \in \mathcal{T _x\mathbb{H _\kappa^n$ with $v \neq \mathbf{0}$ and $y \neq x$, there exists a unique geodesic $\gamma: [0,1] \to \mathbb{H _\kappa^n$ such that $\gamma(0) = x$ and $\gamma^\prime(0) = v$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The exponential map $\exp_x: \mathcal{T _x\mathbb{H _\kappa^n \to \mathbb{H _\kappa^n$ is given by $\exp_{x (v) = \gamma(1)$: \begin{equation}\exp_{x}^{\kappa}(v) = \cosh \left( \frac{\|v\|_{\mathcal{L}}}{\sqrt{\kappa}} \right) x + \sqrt{\kappa} \sinh \left( \frac{\|v\|_\mathcal{L}}{\sqrt{\kappa}} \right) \frac{v}{\|v\|_{\mathcal{L}}}, \label{eq:exp}\end{equation} where $\|v\|_\mathcal{L = \sqrt{\langle v, v \rangle_\mathcal{L }$ denotes the Lorentzian norm of $v$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: The logarithmic map $\log_x$ is the inverse of the exponential map $\exp_x$: \begin{equation}\log_{x}^{\kappa}(y) = d_{\mathcal{L}}^\kappa(x, y) \frac{y + \frac{1}{\kappa} \langle x, y \rangle_\mathcal{L} x}{\|y + \frac{1}{\kappa} \langle x, y \rangle_\mathcal{L} x\|},\end{equation} where $d_\mathcal{H ^\kappa(x, y)$ represents the distance between $x$ and $y$ in $\mathbb{H ^n_\kappa$: \begin{equation}d_\mathcal{H}^\kappa(x, y) = \sqrt{\kappa} \, \text{arcosh} \left( -\langle x, y \rangle_\mathcal{L}/\kappa \right).\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-069: We choose $\mathbf{o := \{\sqrt{\kappa , 0, \ldots, 0\} \in \mathbb{H ^n_\kappa$ as the reference point for all the operations, making the transitions reproducible.}}}

elements.sent-2411-072: In this section, we present a comprehensive overview of our proposed model, \model.
elements.sent-2411-073: Our approach not only aims to deliver accurate recommendations but also addresses users' growing demand for more diverse suggestions.
elements.sent-2411-074: Furthermore, by constructing a hierarchical tree from the learned embeddings, we introduce an additional mechanism that enables users to control the degree of exploration in the recommended items.
elements.sent-2411-075: The overall framework is in Figure \ref{fig:framework .}

(i) Hyperbolic Graph Collaborative Filtering: Encodes collaborative information using hyperbolic GNNs; (ii) Hyperbolic Alignment: Aligns semantic and collaborative information within hyperbolic space; (iii) Hyperbolic Hierarchical Structure: Builds hierarchy tree from hyperbolic embeddings.} \label{fig:framework}
elements.sent-2411-076: Similar to Euclidean space, hyperbolic graph collaborative filtering captures high-order dependencies between users and items via a message aggregation mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-077: In our method, we use the Lorentz representation for both users and items.. Hyperbolic Embedding Initialization.
elements.sent-2411-078: As discussed in section 3, we fix the origin $\mathbf{o := \{\sqrt{\kappa , 0, \ldots, 0\} \in \mathbb{H ^n_\kappa$ and use it as the reference point.}}}
elements.sent-2411-079: We adopt the Gaussian sampling method for the initialization of embeddings in Euclidean space, after that, by injecting them into the tangent space of the reference point, we derive the initial hyperbolic embeddings of both users and items: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} z_u^0 = (0, e_u), \quad\qquad z_i^0 = (0, e_i) h_u^0 = \exp_\mathbf{o}(z_u^0), \quad\quad h_i^0 = \exp_\mathbf{o}(z_i^0) \end{aligned} \label{equ: initialization}\end{equation} where $e_u, e_i$ are Gaussian distributed initialized Euclidean embeddings, and $z_u^0, z_i^0$ are corresponding embeddings in the tangent space of the origin point.

elements.sent-2411-080: Message passing between each node is realized through hyperbolic neighborhood aggregation, which is used to extract explicit user-item interaction.
elements.sent-2411-081: Given user $u$, item $i$, as well as their neighborhood $\mathcal{N _u, \mathcal{N _i$, their embeddings at layer $l$ in the tangent space are updated as follows: \begin{equation}z_u^{l} = z_u^{l-1} + \sum_{i\in \mathcal{N}_u}\frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_u|}z_i^{l-1}, \quad\quad z_i^{l} = z_i^{l-1} + \sum_{u\in \mathcal{N}_i}\frac{1}{|\mathcal{N}_i|}z_u^{l-1}\end{equation} where $|\mathcal{N _u|$ and $|\mathcal{N _i|$ represent the number of neighbors of $u$ and $i$, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: The final tangent space embedding is derived by summing up embeddings at each layer: \begin{equation}z_u = \sum_{l} z_u^l, \quad\quad z_i = \sum_{l} z_i^l\end{equation} Since $z_u$ and $z_i$ are on the tangent space, we still need to project it into hyperbolic space to derive the hyperbolic embeddings: \begin{equation}h_u=\exp_\mathbf{o}(z_u), \quad\quad h_i=\exp_\mathbf{o}(z_i)\end{equation} Hyperbolic Prediction Function.
elements.sent-2411-083: Through hyperbolic messaging passing, we've captured high-order relational information into hyperbolic embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-084: Hyperbolic distance is now used to define a prediction function, which is used to predict the possibility that a user would interact with an item: \begin{equation}p(u,i) = \frac{1}{d_\mathcal{H}(h_u,h_i)}\end{equation} Hyperbolic Margin Ranking Loss.
elements.sent-2411-085: Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) loss has been widely applied in recommender systems~\cite{rendle2012bpr , aiming to pull together user-item pairs with recorded interactions while pushing apart those without.}
elements.sent-2411-086: In our model, we adopt the hyperbolic margin ranking loss~\cite{sun2021hgcf , designed to prevent the undesirable collapse seen in Euclidean space by adding an additional margin to the original BPR loss: \begin{equation}\ell_{margin}(u,i,j) = \max(d_\mathcal{H}^2(h_u, h_i) - d_\mathcal{H}^2(h_u, h_j) + m, 0),\end{equation} where $d_\mathcal{H}$ represents hyperbolic distance, $m$ is the margin, $(u,i)$ is an observed interaction, and $(u,j)$ is a sampled negative pair.. Hyperbolic Informative Mechanism.}
elements.sent-2411-087: We further integrate a geometric-aware mechanism~\cite{yang2022hicf to enhance the pull-push process, consisting of two parts: Hyperbolic-aware Margin Learning (HAML) and Hyperbolic Informative Negative Sampling (HINS).}
elements.sent-2411-088: Given that distances in hyperbolic space grow exponentially as embeddings deviate from the origin, we aim to assign a larger margin when user-item pairs are near the origin.
elements.sent-2411-089: HAML addresses this issue by dynamically adapting the margin as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} m^\mathcal{H}_{ui} = \mathrm{sigmoid}(\delta), \delta = \frac{d_\mathcal{H}^2(e_u, \mathbf{o}) + d_\mathcal{H}^2(e_i, \mathbf{o}) - d_\mathcal{H}^2(e_u, e_i)}{e_{u,0} \cdot e_{i,0}} \end{aligned}\end{equation} where $e_{u,0}$ and $e_{i,0}$ represent the zeroth coordinate elements of $e_u$ and $e_i$, respectively, corresponding to the embeddings norms in the hyperbolic space.

elements.sent-2411-090: HINS is an algorithm that samples negative nodes close to the positive item, yielding informative negative samples for both head and tail items.

elements.sent-2411-092: In this subsection, we introduce a paradigm for distilling useful information from raw text descriptions of items and users into dense semantic embeddings, which will be used later in our alignment framework that derives the final embeddings, integrating both collaborative information and semantic insights.
elements.sent-2411-093: Inspired by prior research~\cite{ma2024xrec, ren2024representation that leverages the power of large language models (LLMs) to extract useful user/item descriptions from raw, noisy textual data, we adopt a similar framework to generate user/item profiles with rich textual descriptions: \begin{equation}\mathcal{P}_i = LLMs(\mathcal{S}_i, \mathcal{T}_i)\end{equation} where $\mathcal{S _i$ represents system prompts to the LLMs, $\mathcal{T _i$ denotes raw textual information, and $\mathcal{P _i$ is the resulting user/item profile, consisting of sentence-level descriptions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-094: While we've derived textual information for users/items, it cannot be directly fused with the embeddings generated from hyperbolic collaborative filtering.
elements.sent-2411-095: To integrate both types of knowledge, we encode the textual information into embeddings using pretrained text encoder models, such as BERT~\cite{devlin2018bert : \begin{equation}e_i = Encoder(\mathcal{P}_i)\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-096: Given two sets of embeddings: hyperbolic embeddings with collaborative information and semantic embeddings with textual information, we aim to maximize the mutual information between them, creating unified embeddings that integrate both types of knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-097: To achieve this, we introduce a hyperbolic alignment loss, which mitigates the effects of noisy signals in representation learning and improves recommendations for tail items by aligning information within the same hyperbolic space.
elements.sent-2411-098: A theoretical proof of the advantages of alignment in hyperbolic space over Euclidean space is provided in the next subsection.
elements.sent-2411-099: Before calculating the alignment loss, two steps are necessary: 1) the semantic embeddings must be adjusted to match the dimensionality of the collaborative embeddings, and 2) they should be transformed into a common representation space.
elements.sent-2411-100: To accomplish this, we first apply a multi-layer perceptron (MLP) to the semantic embeddings to ensure dimensional alignment.

elements.sent-2411-113: In this subsection, we introduce a mechanism that allows users to control the level of exploration in their recommendations by constructing a hierarchy tree based on the learned hyperbolic embeddings, an example is shown in Figure \ref{fig:hierarchy .}
elements.sent-2411-114: These embeddings integrate both collaborative information and language semantics, enabling more nuanced recommendations.. Hierarchy Tree.
elements.sent-2411-115: The primary motivation for constructing a hierarchy tree is to uncover hidden structures in the data that are not explicitly reflected in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-116: For instance, consider the Amazon-CD dataset: a CD might be categorized under genres like ``folk music'', but it cannot be labeled as ``folk music preferred by teenagers'' or further refined to ``folk music preferred by teenagers who live in large cities''.
elements.sent-2411-117: However, in reality, such nuanced preferences exist within the collaborative information.
elements.sent-2411-118: When recommending based solely on past interactions, we may overlook these hidden layers of structure that are implicit in the dataset (e.g., ``music'' → ``folk music'' → ``folk music preferred by teenagers'' → ``folk music preferred by teenagers who live in large cities'').
elements.sent-2411-119: As previously discussed, in hyperbolic space, the norm of the embeddings reflects the level of exploration, where depth search facilitates exploitation and breadth search enables exploration.
elements.sent-2411-120: Building on this concept, we use the learned embeddings to form the bottom layer of the hierarchy tree.

elements.sent-2411-222: Modern recommender systems often create information cocoons, restricting users' exposure to diverse content.
elements.sent-2411-223: A key challenge lies in balancing content exploration and exploitation while allowing users to adjust their recommendation preferences.
elements.sent-2411-224: Intuitively, this balance can be modeled as a tree-structured representation, where depth search facilitates exploitation and breadth search enables exploration.
elements.sent-2411-225: However, existing approaches face two fundamental limitations: Euclidean methods struggle to capture hierarchical structures, while hyperbolic methods, despite their superior hierarchical modeling, lack semantic understanding of user and item profiles and fail to provide a principled mechanism for balancing exploration and exploitation.
elements.sent-2411-226: To address these challenges, we propose \model, a hyperbolic graph-LLM framework that effectively balances exploration and exploitation in recommender systems.
elements.sent-2411-227: Our framework introduces two key innovations: (1) a hierarchical-aware graph-LLM mechanism that jointly aligns textual descriptions with user-item collaborative information in hyperbolic space, and (2) a hierarchical representation structure that enables user-adjustable exploration-exploitation trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-228: Extensive experiments demonstrate that \model consistently outperforms both Euclidean and hyperbolic baselines, achieving up to 5.49\% improvement in utility metrics and 11.39\% increase in diversity metrics, effectively mitigating information cocoons.
elements.sent-2411-229: As online platforms grow in size dramatically, users are overwhelmed by an influx of information, creating information cocoons that restrict their exposure to diverse and novel content~\cite{kunaver2017diversity, castells2021novelty .}
elements.sent-2411-230: First, existing models struggle to capture the underlying hierarchical structure inherent in user-item networks~\cite{zheng2017hierarchical, unger2020hierarchical , a critical factor for enhancing performance and personalization.}
elements.sent-2411-231: Second, these models fail to effectively balance the exploration-exploitation trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-232: For instance, a jazz enthusiast may want to explore rock music, but current models primarily focus on exploitation—recommending only familiar genres—without adequately supporting exploration (as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:Exp ).}
elements.sent-2411-233: Hyperbolic space has emerged as a promising approach for modeling hierarchical structures and power-law distributions~\cite{krioukov2010hyperbolic,yang2022hrcf,sun2021hgcf,yang2022hicf , where its exponential volume growth naturally aligns with hierarchical, scale-free structures~\cite{ganea2018hyperbolic, chami2019hyperbolic, liu2019hyperbolic, zhang2021lorentzian, yang2023kappahgcn .}}
elements.sent-2411-234: This spatial configuration enables hyperbolic graph neural network (GNN) models to perceive hierarchical relationships effectively, enhancing recommendation performance~\cite{yang2022hicf, sun2021hgcf, yang2022hrcf .}
elements.sent-2411-235: Despite these advantages, current hyperbolic recommender systems still face key limitations.
elements.sent-2411-236: First, they struggle with a lack of semantic understanding of user and item profiles.
elements.sent-2411-237: While large language models (LLMs) offer a potential solution, their text encoders operate in Euclidean space, making it challenging to integrate LLM-derived semantic features with hyperbolic collaborative signals.
elements.sent-2411-238: This mismatch leaves existing hyperbolic models vulnerable to noise in collaborative information, as they fail to capture semantic insights effectively.
elements.sent-2411-239: Second, hyperbolic recommender systems lack a principled approach to balancing exploration (introducing new content) and exploitation (focusing on existing preferences)~\cite{wilson2021balancing .}
elements.sent-2411-240: This limitation reduces their ability to adapt to the exploration-exploitation trade-off, ultimately diminishing both the utility and diversity of recommendations.. Proposed Methods.
elements.sent-2411-241: To address these challenges, we propose \model, a hyperbolic graph-LLM framework designed to balance exploration and exploitation in recommender systems.
elements.sent-2411-242: Our approach introduces two key innovations: (1) a hierarchical-aware graph-LLM alignment mechanism, which jointly aligns textual descriptions with user-item collaborative information in hyperbolic space, supported by theoretical gradient analysis demonstrating its advantages, and (2) a novel hierarchical representation structure that enables user-adjustable exploration-exploitation trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-243: Unlike conventional approaches that require manually defined hierarchies, we propose a hyperparameter-free clustering mechanism theoretically optimized by Dasgupta’s cost~\cite{dasgupta2016cost .}
elements.sent-2411-244: This approach automatically discovers hierarchical structures without requiring predefined hyperparameters, allowing for a more adaptive and principled organization of user preferences.
elements.sent-2411-245: Our model consistently outperformed baselines in both \acc and diversity metrics, marking a significant achievement as it is the first model to excel in both aspects simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-246: For a fair comparison, we enhanced Euclidean baselines with feature information, yet our model still demonstrated superior performance, validating the advantage of our designed alignment in hyperbolic space.
elements.sent-2411-247: Additionally, we conducted a detailed analysis on head and tail items, showing that \model effectively boosts recommendations for tail items, thus enhancing diversity.
elements.sent-2411-248: An ablation study, along with a hierarchical representation structure analysis and decoder module, confirms \model's capability to leverage both semantic meaning and collaborative information, providing deeper insights into the advantages of our approach.. Contributions.
elements.sent-2411-249: Our primary contributions focus on three key aspects: capturing collaborative signals, integrating semantic information, and modeling the underlying hierarchical structure.
elements.sent-2411-250: The proposed graph-LLM alignment framework unifies collaborative and semantic information in hyperbolic space, while a hierarchical representation structure organizes user preferences, allowing for dynamic adjustment between exploration and exploitation.

elements.sent-2411-295: To address this, we introduce a hyperbolic hierarchical clustering method based on hyperbolic embeddings to build the hierarchy tree.

elements.sent-2411-300: The details of the algorithm are presented in Algorithm \ref{alg:cluster .}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Hyperbolic Hierarchical Clustering} \label{alg:cluster} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} Hyperbolic embeddings $X$, layer proportion $k$ \STATE Set the maximum number of layers $L = \log_k(|X|)$ \STATE Initialize the points of bottom layer $D_L = X$ \FOR{$l = L$ { to} $1$ (bottom to top)} \STATE Randomly initialize cluster centroids in the hyperbolic space $M_l = \{\mu_l\ |\ \mu_l\in \mathbb{H}\}$, $|M_l| = |D_l|/k$ \REPEAT \FOR{each point $d_{l,i} \in D_l$} \STATE Assign $d_i$ to the cluster with the nearest centroid: \STATE $c_{l,i} = \arg\min_{\mu \in M_l} d_\mathcal{H}(d_i,\mu)$ \ENDFOR \FOR{each cluster $c_{l,i} \in C_l$} \STATE Update the centroid $\mu_{l,i}$ as the hyperbolic mean $m_{\mathcal{H}}$ of all points assigned to cluster $c_{l,i}$: \STATE $\mu_{l,i} = m_{\mathcal{H}}(\{d\ |\ d\in C_{l,i}\})$ \ENDFOR \UNTIL Centroids $M_l$ do not change (convergence) \STATE Initialize the points of the upper layer $D_{l-1} = M_l$ \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-347: In this work, we present \model, a hyperbolic graph-LLM framework that effectively balances exploration and exploitation in recommender systems.
elements.sent-2411-348: Our approach integrates semantic and collaborative information within hyperbolic space, leveraging the unique properties of hyperbolic geometry.
elements.sent-2411-349: Furthermore, we introduce a personalized mechanism for balancing the exploration-exploitation trade-off using a hierarchical representation structure, empowering users to adjust recommendation preferences flexibly.

elements.sent-2411-458: In this section, we provide details of the hyperbolic hierarchical clustering algorithm, together with complexity analysis and a detailed case study.
elements.sent-2411-459: Algorithm~\ref{alg:cluster describes the hyperbolic hierarchical clustering procedure used to organize hyperbolic embeddings into a multi-layer structure.}
elements.sent-2411-460: The algorithm takes as input the hyperbolic embeddings $X$ and a layer proportion parameter $k$, which controls the reduction rate of points per hierarchical layer.
elements.sent-2411-461: The maximum number of layers $L$ is set to $\log_k(|X|)$, ensuring that each subsequent layer contains approximately $1/k$ times the points of the previous layer.
elements.sent-2411-462: Starting from the bottom layer containing all points, the algorithm iteratively constructs higher layers in a bottom-up fashion.
elements.sent-2411-463: At each layer $l$, cluster centroids are randomly initialized in hyperbolic space, with the number of clusters equal to $|D_l|/k$, where $|D_l|$ is the number of points in the current layer.
elements.sent-2411-464: Clustering proceeds by repeatedly assigning each point to its nearest centroid (in terms of hyperbolic distance) and updating each centroid to the hyperbolic mean of its assigned points.
elements.sent-2411-465: This process continues until the centroids converge.
elements.sent-2411-466: Upon convergence, the centroids from the current layer serve as the points for the next upper layer.
elements.sent-2411-467: By repeating this process, the algorithm constructs a hierarchy of clusters, effectively capturing both the geometric structure and hierarchical relationships inherent in hyperbolic embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-468: In this subsection, we analyze the complexity of constructing and applying the hierarchical representation structure.
elements.sent-2411-469: Let $N$ be the total number of users and items, and $k$ be the number of recommendations per user.. Hierarchical Clustering.
elements.sent-2411-470: Building the hierarchy tree involves iterative k-means clustering in hyperbolic space.
elements.sent-2411-471: To optimize efficiency, we assign cluster nodes only to real node embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-472: First, we compute and store pairwise distances, requiring $O(N^2/2)$.
elements.sent-2411-473: Each k-means iteration takes $O(i)$, and with $O(\log N)$ levels, the overall complexity of constructing the hierarchy tree is $O(N^2/2 + i\log N)$.
elements.sent-2411-475: Finding the top-$k$ recommendations requires $O(k \log N)$ using nearest neighbour search in hyperbolic space.
elements.sent-2411-476: Adjusting recommendations by retrieving ancestor clusters at layer $l$ requires $O(k)$ time, as it involves only tree traversal.
elements.sent-2411-477: The proposed hierarchical framework achieves efficient complexity scaling while maintaining flexibility in recommendation adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-478: The hierarchical clustering process, optimized for hyperbolic space, ensures a computationally feasible structure with $O(N^2/2 + i\log N)$ complexity.
elements.sent-2411-479: The recommendation retrieval and replacement steps remain lightweight, running in $O(k \log N)$ and $O(k)$, respectively.
